rationale_for_baseline_tier: A large local reaction to an insect bite with no systemic features is benign and self-managed with an antihistamine, topical steroid and cold compresses, with routine follow-up (T1). Anaphylaxis carries a hard T5 safety-net; secondary cellulitis over the following days routes to GP/ED (T4).

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Breathing difficulty, swelling of lips/tongue/throat, whole-body hives, or dizziness/faintness
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get any trouble breathing, swelling of your lips/tongue/throat, a whole-body rash, or feel faint — call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Over the next days: increasing pain and spreading redness, a red streak up the leg, or fever/chills
escalation_edges.communication_template: If over the next few days the area becomes increasingly painful and red, a red line travels up your leg, or you get a fever — see a doctor / go to ED that day.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Provide clear anaphylaxis and cellulitis red flags for self-monitoring.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Take a non-drowsy antihistamine, use the steroid cream and cold packs, and try not to scratch. It should settle over the next day or two.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Self-care; 000 for anaphylaxis; GP/ED if cellulitis develops
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 for any breathing/systemic features at any time.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Large local reaction to insect bite; anaphylaxis and cellulitis screened negative; antihistamine + topical steroid.

critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A delayed systemic reaction is not recognised as an emergency and progresses to anaphylaxis.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: No cellulitis safety-net over the following days.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: ED/ambulance or antibiotics for a benign fresh itchy bite — unnecessary.
